data.title: Annica Holdings to purchase waste tyre plant in Malaysia
data.description:
The total consideration for the acquisition is $2.7m.

Annica Holdings has entered into a conditional asset purchase agreement to acquire GPL Industries' waste tyre pyrolysis plant in Malaysia.

The total consideration for the acquisition is $2.7m. It will be satisfied by allotting and issuing 1.7 billion shares in the company's capital for $0.0015 per share.

The acquisition is part of the group's strategy to diversify into recycling, renewable energy, and green technology businesses.

data.title: Hongkong Land widens loss by 138% in FY2024
data.description:
It was due to non-cash provisions from the Chinese mainland build-to-sell business.

Hongkong Land Holdings Limited has reported its net loss attributable to shareholders widened by 138% year-on-year to $10.8b (US$1.39b) for the financial year 2024 ended 31 December.

The group’s underlying profit attributable to shareholders for the period came in at $3.2b (US$410m), down 44% YoY.

It attributed the decline to non-cash provisions from the Chinese mainland build-to-sell business.

In Hong Kong, physical vacancy was 7.3%  whilst committed basis vacancy was 7.1%. The group's average portfolio office rent was $101 per square foot.

Moreover, the group’s investment properties portfolio in Hong Kong declined by 5% to $177b (US$22.8b) due to a decline in market rent for offices.

The group has proposed a final dividend of $1.32 (US$0.17) per share.

($1=US$0.13)

data.title: Singapore eyes 32 polyclinics by 2030
data.description:
Five new facilities are scheduled for completion by 2027.

Singapore is set to expand its polyclinic network from 26 to 32 by 2030, with new facilities and redevelopment projects planned.

Five new polyclinics in Bidadari, Kaki Bukit, Serangoon, Tengah, and Yew Tee are scheduled for completion by 2027.

Meanwhile, two more in Bishan and Taman Jurong are expected to be completed by 2030.

In addition, six existing facilities—Bukit Merah, Clementi, Jurong, Outram, Queenstown, and Toa Payoh—will undergo redevelopment by 2030 to improve services and infrastructure.

data.title: NDCS launches oral screening tool on Health Buddy app
data.description:
It assesses the risk of severe gum disease and oral frailty.

The National Dental Centre Singapore has introduced an oral health screening tool on SingHealth’s Health Buddy application.

The new tool allows users to assess their risk of severe gum disease and oral frailty through a self-assessment questionnaire.

The platform consists of a simplified nomogram for gum disease that includes six questions to predict the risk of the disease.

It also includes the Oral Frailty Index-8 which features eight questions to evaluate oral frailty risk in adults.

data.title: Gov’t to inject $1.2b to fund enterprises' growth
data.description:
It will introduce two new funding programmes for enterprises.

The government will inject a total of $1.2b to support enterprises' growth through two new financing instruments—the Private Credit Growth Fund (PCGF) and the Long Term Investment Fund (LTIF).

The Ministry for Trade and Industry and Enterprise Singapore will inject $1b into the PCGF which is suitable for high-growth enterprises that require nondilutive financing solutions.

More details about the fund, including the fund manager and eligibility criteria, will be announced by the third quarter of the year.

Additionally, the government will provide more than $200m for the LTIF which will make investments beyond the typical three- to seven-year term.

It is suitable for growth enterprises with longer and more complex growth trajectories that require highly patient capital. More details about the fund will be announced by the second half of the year.

data.title: Lentor Central sells over 93% of launched units
data.description:
The one- and two-bedroom unit types accounted for about 56% of the sales.

Lentor Central Residences has sold 93% or 445 units out of the 477 units launched at prices ranging from $1,982 to $2,573 per square foot.

“The one and two-bedroom unit types accounted for about 56% of the sales whilst the three and four-bedders made up the rest. Notably, all the three-bedroom units are fully taken up,” Ismail Gafoor, chief executive officer of PropNex, said.

Approximately 90% of the buyers were Singaporeans whilst permanent residents and foreigners accounted for the remaining 10%.

data.title: Aurea sells 30% of launched units
data.description:
The two- and three-bedroom units account for 74% of the sales.

Golden Mile Singapore’s Aurea residence has sold 30% of the 78 units it launched at an average price of $3,005 per square foot.

The two- and three-bedroom apartments from the Prestige Collection accounted for 74% of the sales.

Most, or 83%, of Aurea homebuyers are Singaporeans whilst the remaining 17% are Malaysian residents.

data.title: Singapore banks see stable credit costs and resilient earnings: RHB
data.description:
The sector has had a strong start to the year, outperforming ASEAN peers as its defensive nature and attractive dividend yields continue to attract investors.

RHB maintains an Overweight rating on Singapore banks, with DBS, United Overseas Bank (UOB), and OCBC Bank (OCBC) as top picks.

In its report, RHB said the sector has had a strong start to the year, outperforming ASEAN peers as its defensive nature and attractive dividend yields continue to attract investors.

Capital return plans from the banks have further reinforced this positive outlook, with DBS standing out for its “too-good-to-ignore” FY25F dividend yield.

4Q24 results were in line with expectations, with weaker sequential earnings largely due to seasonality and an elevated non-interest income (Non-II) base from 3Q24. Whilst lower Non-II and higher expenses led to a 16% QoQ drop in profit before tax (PIOP), PATMI still grew 8% YoY, underscoring the sector’s resilience.

Loan credit costs remained stable at 23bps, while net interest income (NII) ticked up 2% QoQ (+4% YoY), helped by asset growth and, in DBS' case, improved NIM from lower funding costs.

Singapore banks have retained their 2025 guidance, with DBS and UOB expecting modest loan growth, while OCBC guides for a sharper 20bps NIM squeeze, partly due to its assumption of more aggressive rate cuts by the US Federal Reserve.

All three banks unveiled capital return plans, with DBS planning to return SGD 8bn, OCBC SGD 2.5bn, and UOB SGD 3bn over the next 2-3 years via a mix of dividends and share buybacks.

Sector earnings forecasts have been revised upwards, with FY25F-26F PATMI raised by 2-3%. While FY25F sector earnings are expected to stay flat, this is largely due to the impact of the global minimum tax rate, which offsets projected 2% PIOP growth and stable credit costs.

Non-II declined 23% QoQ but was up 11% YoY, reflecting seasonality but also strong underlying growth. CASA deposits continued to rise, with UOB seeing the biggest gains, whilst asset quality remained broadly stable despite a slight rise in non-performing loans (NPLs).

Loan loss coverage eased to 114% from 121% in 3Q24, but RHB notes that provisioning buffers remain strong.

DBS remains the preferred pick, with a target price of SGD 51.20, followed by UOB at SGD 41.60 and OCBC at SGD 19.10.

Whilst FY25F earnings growth may be limited, RHB believes Singapore banks will continue to provide an attractive risk-reward proposition in the face of market volatility.

data.title: What pushes real estate salaries higher?
data.description:
Real estate professionals earn higher salaries after promotions or job changes.

In Singapore, annual pay reviews and promotions are the top drivers of salary increases in the real estate sector, according to Macdonald & Company.

Cost of living and inflation follow, along with new jobs, work or structural changes, and professional qualifications.

However, most professionals saw higher pay when they were promoted or switched jobs.

In addition, professional qualifications and annual pay reviews were the least common reasons for salary increases.

data.title: Daily Markets Briefing: STI down 0.07%; Top stock is UOL
data.description:
UOL is the top stock with a 1.57% increase.

The Straits Times Index (STI) closed at 3,914.480 down 0.07% on Friday, 7 March, at 5:21 p.m.

UOL is the most active stock, followed by DFIRG USD (+1.44%) and Singtel (+0.89%)

SIA and CapitaLand IntCom T were at the foot.
